name: TechEmpower — Single Database Query
notes: TFB single-query test: one indexed single-row read of the World table per request, serialized as JSON, at 512 concurrency. A pure capacity probe. SDA models it client.web -> compute.service (the web framework) -> db.postgres. The scored metric is the throughput ceiling (client req/s where the busiest tier reaches rho=1). Two SEPARATE resources are now fitted: db.postgres.perRequestDuration (SHARED with the multi-query entry) AND the framework's CPU station (compute.service.cpuCores + cpuTimePerRequestMs). With a distinct CPU resource on the framework the single-query ceiling is CPU-bound (framework) while the 20-query ceiling is DB-bound, so the joint fit can satisfy BOTH exact — the +13% single-query residual that the old DB-only model could not remove (no CPU primitive) now CLOSES. cpuCores is PINNED to the 28 hardware threads of the Round-22 Citrine app box (the same Xeon Gold 5120, 14c/28t as the DB box); cpuTimePerRequestMs is the fitted per-request CPU time (capacity = cores/cpuTime, so we pin the sourced cores and fit the time). db concurrency is likewise PINNED to 28.

sources.note: TFB test-requirements wiki: single-query = one random-id World row read, serialized as JSON, peak reported at 512 concurrency
Round 22 (Run ID 66d86090, Oct 2023) on the Citrine environment: DB box = Intel Xeon Gold 5120, 14c/28t
Citrine hardware environment (three Dell R440, dedicated 10 GbE): one DB box with 28 hardware threads

tunables.note: PostgreSQL per-query service time. Catalog default 50 ms is a mixed-OLTP query; the TFB World read is a cache-resident indexed single-row read (~0.24 ms). The dominant out-of-box miss (~210x). SHARED with the 20-query entry — the 20-query point anchors it (118,000 q/s = 5,900 x 20); at single-query the DB no longer has to also explain the ceiling because the framework CPU now binds first.

Parallel-query slots. Catalog default 100 = PostgreSQL max_connections; PINNED to 28 = the DB box's hardware threads (true parallelism for a CPU-bound cache-resident read). Not fitted — capacity = concurrency/perRequestDuration, so the ceiling is degenerate along the ratio; we pin the sourced hardware fact and fit the service time.

The web framework's CPU width. PINNED to 28 = the Citrine app box's hardware threads (Round-22 environment = three identical Dell R440, Intel Xeon Gold 5120 14c/28t; the app server is the same class as the DB box). Not fitted — CPU capacity = cores/cpuTime is degenerate along the ratio, so we pin the sourced hardware and fit the per-request CPU time.

The framework's per-request CPU time (parse + route + query issue + JSON serialize). A fittable ESTIMATE (no published per-request CPU figure). Catalog default 1 ms is a generous, uncalibrated placeholder (framework does not bind out of the box); the fit drives it so cores/cpuTime = the measured 104,000 req/s single-query ceiling. SHARED with the 20-query entry but INERT there (the DB, at ceiling/20 = 5,900, binds far below the framework's CPU), so it is constrained only by this single-query point — the framework-CPU degree of freedom the DB-only model lacked.

groundTruth.note: spring-mongo single-query peak, Round 22 baseline quoted in TechEmpower's engineering blog 'How Our Benchmarks Led to a 25x MongoDB Performance Improvement' (2026-03-16). The DB rate is anchored by the 20-query point (118,000 q/s), so at single-query the DB alone would over-predict (~+13%): reality is CPU-bound at one query/request. With a SEPARATE framework CPU station (cpuCores/cpuTimePerRequestMs) SDA now expresses that resource, so the fit binds the single-query ceiling on the framework CPU (104,000) and the 20-query ceiling on the DB (5,900) — both exact. This is the calibration TEST: does modelling CPU close the +13% single-query gap? (Externally corroborated: TechEmpower later lifted this result 25x with framework-side fixes, never touching the DB.)
